title: Accountability Engine
content: Accountability Engine — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Accountability Engine The Accountability Engine is the reasoning core of .Aayan-Mem. It is designed to slow down the agent's impulse to code and force a rigorous analysis of the architectural impact. Located in: .Aayan-Mem/Agent/ The Purpose of Accountability Traditional AI agents often fall into "vibe-based" coding, where they choose the first implementation path that comes to mind. The Accountability Engine eliminates this by enforcing: Forced Simulation: Before writing code, the agent must simulate the execution path. Auditability: Every decision is saved as a Markdown file, creating a permanent, human-readable history of the project's evolution. Contradiction Avoidance: Decisions must be validated against the Recall Engine to ensure they don't violate existing rules. The Decision Matrix For any task classified as Tier 3 (Architecture) , the agent must generate a Decision Matrix . This matrix compares multiple paths based on complexity, performance, and risk metrics. Components Component Role decision-logs/ Storage for every architectural choice made in the project. session-template.md The canonical structure for high-quality decision logging. decision-index.md A lightweight index for fast pointer searching across session logs. session-counter.json Tracks the current session ID to ensure unique log filenames. tools/ The logic layer for search, validation, and diagnostics. Operating Rules The Accountability Engine is governed by the 8 Operational Rules . These rules ensure that the agent remains tethered to reality and follows the documented decision timeline. Previous Quick Start Next Recall Engine

title: Agent Integration
content: Agent Integration — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Agent Integration Learn how to "teach" any AI agent (Claude, GPT-4, Gemini) to utilize the .Aayan-Mem framework effectively. System Prompt Injection The easiest way to integrate .Aayan-Mem is to include a brief instruction in your agent's system prompt or "Custom Instructions": "You are a Senior Engineer working with the .Aayan-Mem framework. Before any task, run `node a-mem coldstart`. Document all Tier 3 architectural decisions in `.Aayan-Mem/Agent/decision-logs/`. Use `node a-mem find` for all codebase queries." Supported Agents Claude (Anthropic): Exceptional at following the 8 Operational Rules. GPT-4o (OpenAI): Highly efficient with the `find` tool and snippet parsing. Gemini 1.5 Pro: Excellent for managing the large Context-Index and long-term goals. Prompting Technique: "Proof of Read" One of the core features of .Aayan-Mem is the Proof-of-Read rule. After running a command, the agent should echo back 3 specific facts it learned from the memory briefing to prove it has ingested the context. Example: "I have completed the coldstart. Fact 1: The project uses Supabase. Fact 2: Glassmorphism is the primary UI style. Fact 3: We are currently in Session 14." Previous Security & Privacy Next A-MEM vs RAG

title: Best Practices
content: Best Practices — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Best Practices To get the most out of .Aayan-Mem , you and your agent must maintain a high level of "Memory Discipline." Follow these guidelines to ensure architectural stability. 1. The Coldstart Mandate Never allow an agent to skip the node a-mem coldstart command at the beginning of a session. This ensures the agent is working with the latest "ground truth" and hasn't drifted into following old architectural patterns. 2. Atomic Decision Logging Don't group multiple large features into a single session log. Each new "title" should represent an atomic unit of work. This makes the log command much more useful for future audits. 3. Precision in Memory Files Keep your Memory-&-Context/ files concise. Use bullet points and headers. Avoid long-form paragraphs that might confuse the agent's keyword-based find search. 4. Regular Validation Run node a-mem status at least once every three sessions. It acts as a "unit test for your memory," catching broken file paths or logic contradictions before they become embedded in the codebase. Rule of Thumb: If you find yourself explaining the same thing to an AI twice, that information belongs in Recall Engine/rules.md . Previous Operational Rules Next Token Optimization

title: Command List
content: Command List — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Command List (v2.0.0) The a-mem CLI is the primary bridge between the AI agent and the memory framework. All commands should be executed from the project root using node a-mem . Core Workflow Command Description node a-mem init Initialization. Sets up the initial Memory-&-Context files and Agent directory structure for a new project. node a-mem coldstart Session Start. Reads core memory briefings and scans for stale decisions needing review. Essential for context loading. node a-mem new "title" Decision Logging. Creates a new session log with the specified title, pre-filled with the decision template. node a-mem close [N] Session Closure. Closes session N and runs Post-Implementation Verification (PIV) to ensure changes meet rules. node a-mem sync Index Rebuild. Rebuilds the decision-index.md to ensure the agent has an up-to-date map of all past choices. Discovery & Diagnostics Command Description node a-mem find "term" Surgical Search. Searches all memory files with a 4-line context buffer to prevent token leaks. node a-mem status System Health. Runs all diagnostic checks (path verification, contradiction scanning) at once. node a-mem log Decision Timeline. Prints a chronological history of architectural choices from the Accountability Engine. node a-mem help Reference. Displays the integrated help menu with usage examples. Previous Decision Matrices Next Operational Rules

title: A-MEM vs RAG
content: A-MEM vs RAG — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K A-MEM vs RAG While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is popular for codebase Q&A, .Aayan-Mem offers a more deterministic approach for agentic engineering. The Key Difference Standard RAG systems treat your codebase as a "bag of words" to be embedded and retrieved. .Aayan-Mem treats your codebase as a living logic system to be audited. Feature Traditional RAG .Aayan-Mem Retrieval Style Semantic / Similarity Keyword / Deterministic Hallucination Risk Moderate (Vector "noise") Zero (Path verification) Infrastructure Vector DB, Embeddings Markdown, Node.js Decision History None Permanent (Agent Logs) Cost High (API + Hosting) $0 (Local) Why use .Aayan-Mem over RAG? RAG is excellent for finding *where* something is, but it's terrible at explaining *why* it was built that way. .Aayan-Mem records the "Why" via the Accountability Engine , preventing the agent from repeating architectural mistakes that were already solved in previous sessions. Previous Agent Integration Next Command List

title: Features
content: Features — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Key Features Explore the advanced capabilities of .Aayan-Mem that make it the industry standard for AI memory management. Zero Token Leaks Strict 4-line context buffers for snippet searches ensure you never waste tokens on massive file dumps. Hallucination Guard Real-time file path verification prevents agents from referencing non-existent files or directories. Decision Matrices Automated Tier 3 simulations force agents to compare 3 architectural paths before committing code. Contradiction Engine Scans all memory files to detect conflicting rules or architectural patterns instantly. Stale Logic Scanner Flags architectural decisions that are past their review date, ensuring the agent never follows outdated logic. Zero Dependency Runs purely on Node.js. No vector databases, no API keys, no hidden costs. Pure local performance. Previous Quick Start Next Accountability Engine

title: Stopping Hallucinations
content: Stopping Hallucinations — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Stopping Hallucinations Hallucination is the single biggest failure point of AI agents. .Aayan-Mem was designed from the ground up to solve this using a physical and logical "tethering" system. Definition: In .Aayan-Mem, a hallucination is defined as any agent claim that contradicts the physical state of the disk or the logical state of the Recall Engine. 1. The Intent vs. Reality Gap Most agents fail because they confuse Memory (what they intended to do) with Reality (what is actually on disk). .Aayan-Mem implements a "Hallucination Guard" to bridge this gap. Physical Verification (The Guard) Whenever an agent references a file path in its reasoning, it is legally required to run node a-mem verify . This tool doesn't just check the memory—it physically queries the operating system's filesystem. # The agent must prove the path exists before referencing it node a-mem verify 2. The Two-Brain Separation Hallucinations often happen when an agent's "Working Memory" becomes polluted with its own guesses. .Aayan-Mem prevents this by physically separating memory into two distinct hemispheres: Recall Engine (The Library): Static, immutable facts about the project. The agent reads this but cannot easily modify it without validation. Accountability Engine (The Workroom): Where active reasoning happens. Reasoning is kept here and only committed to memory after it has been verified. 3. The Proof-of-Read Protocol As part of Rule #6 , agents must echo back 3 key facts after a coldstart . This forces the agent's attention mechanism to actually process the ground truth rather than glossing over it and relying on internal weights. 4. Contradiction Scanning The validate tool is the logical equivalent of a "Unit Test for Facts." It scans for conflicting definitions across the entire memory suite. If the agent hallucinated a rule that contradicts the master rules.md , the scanner flags it as a critical error. 5. Zero-Leak Search By using the find command with a surgical context buffer, the agent never sees enough "irrelevant code" to get confused. By limiting the input to only the necessary snippets, we drastically reduce the surface area for hallucinations. Previous Recall Engine Next Decision Matrices

title: Introduction
content: Introduction — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Introduction Welcome to the official documentation for .Aayan-Mem , a high-performance memory and reasoning framework built to eliminate AI agent hallucinations and "logic drift." Philosophy: AI agents should never "guess." They should verify the ground truth and document their reasoning before executing a single command. The Problem: Vibe-based Coding Most AI coding agents suffer from significant issues when left unconstrained: Hallucinations: Referencing file paths that don't exist or using non-existent library versions. Stale Logic: Following architectural patterns that have already been superseded in the current codebase. Token Leaks: Wasting context limits by reading massive monolithic files instead of surgical snippet searches. The Solution: Two-Brain Architecture .Aayan-Mem solves these problems by splitting the agent's context into two distinct hemispheres: 1. The Recall Engine (Long-term Memory) The Recall Engine acts as the immutable ground truth for the project. It contains the tech stack, directory structure, design guidelines, and quality rules. The agent is physically barred from making assumptions and must instead query this engine for facts. 2. The Accountability Engine (Reasoning) The Accountability Engine forces the agent to simulate, compare, and document its reasoning. Before implementing a complex feature, the agent must generate a Decision Matrix to prove why its chosen path is superior. This lives in the Agent/ directory. Zero-Dependency Design .Aayan-Mem is built for portability. It requires no external databases or API keys, running purely on Node.js and local Markdown files. This ensures that your project memory is as private and fast as your local filesystem. Next Installation

title: Installation
content: Installation — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Installation Setting up .Aayan-Mem is a matter of seconds. As a zero-dependency architecture, it doesn't require complex installation scripts or build steps. Prerequisites Node.js: Version 14.0.0 or higher is required. Terminal: Works in PowerShell, CMD, bash, and zsh. Step 1: Integration Copy the core .Aayan-Mem/ directory and the a-mem.js CLI entry point into the root of your project. # On Unix systems cp -r .Aayan-Mem/ path/to/your/project/ cp a-mem.js path/to/your/project/ # On Windows xcopy /E /I .Aayan-Mem path\to\your\project\.Aayan-Mem copy a-mem.js path\to\your\project\ Step 2: Running Commands The primary way to interact with the framework is by using the node command directly from your project root. This ensures maximum compatibility across all operating systems without needing to configure complex environment paths. Standard Usage node a-mem [command] For example, to initialize your session: node a-mem coldstart Pro Tip: Ensure you are in the root directory where a-mem.js is located before running commands. Security Note: .Aayan-Mem never makes external network requests. All your memory data remains local to your workspace. Previous Introduction Next Quick Start

title: Decision Matrices
content: Decision Matrices — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Decision Matrices A Decision Matrix is a rigorous simulation framework used by .Aayan-Mem to evaluate multiple implementation strategies. It is the primary tool for preventing "vibe-based" architectural drift. Requirement: A full 3-path simulation and comparison matrix is mandatory for all Tier 3 (Architecture) tasks. The Simulation Flow When the agent encounters a complex task, it must follow this flow: Tier Type Requirement Tier 1 Trivial (CSS, Text) Skip simulation. Execute immediately. Tier 2 Feature (Logic) Log chosen path + 1-sentence reasoning. Tier 3 Architecture (Cross-system) Full 3-path simulation matrix required. Identify 3 Paths: Propose three distinct ways to solve the problem (e.g., Path A: Use Library X, Path B: Build Custom Solution, Path C: Refactor Existing Logic). Score Each Path: Rate every path from 1-10 on specific criteria. Document Winner: Write the results to a session log in the decision-logs/ folder. The Scoring Matrix A standard matrix evaluates paths against three core metrics: Complexity: How much technical debt or maintenance burden does this add? Performance: How does it impact latency, tokens, or resource usage? Risk: What is the probability of introducing regressions or security leaks? Example Matrix In a scenario where an agent needs to implement a search feature: | Path | Complexity | Performance | Risk | Total | |------|------------|-------------|------|-------| | A | 4 (Medium) | 7 (High) | 2 | 13 | | B | 8 (High) | 9 (Ultra) | 5 | 22 | | C | 2 (Low) | 5 (Medium) | 1 | 8 | The Decision Log The chosen path must be documented using the session-template.md . This log must include: Context: Why was this task initiated? Rejected Paths: Explicit reasoning for why Path B or C was discarded. Verification Plan: How will we prove the implementation worked? Previous Stopping Hallucinations Next Command List

title: Quick Start
content: Quick Start — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Quick Start Guide Follow these steps to initialize .Aayan-Mem v2.0.0 in your project and start your first high-accountability session. Step 1: Initialize Memory If you are starting a new project, run the init command to generate the template structure. node a-mem init This creates the .Aayan-Mem/ directory with default files like App-Goal.md , Stack.md , and the Agent/ decision log structure. Step 2: Start a Session At the beginning of every coding task, run coldstart to brief yourself (and the agent) on the project context. node a-mem coldstart This command reads the first 5 lines of every memory file and checks for any stale decisions that need review before work begins. Step 3: Log a Decision Before writing code for a new feature, create a decision log. node a-mem new "Implementing Supabase Auth" Open the newly created file in Agent/decision-logs/ and fill out the architectural paths and comparison matrix. Step 4: Verify & Close Once the code is written, run status to check for contradictions, then close the session. node a-mem status node a-mem close 1 Remember: The agent should never bypass the coldstart rule. It is the foundation of the Zero-Hallucination protocol. Previous Installation Next Features

title: Recall Engine
content: Recall Engine — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Recall Engine The Recall Engine provides the "Static Truth" for your project. While the Accountability Engine tracks active reasoning, the Recall Engine holds the immutable rules and architectural mappings. Located in: .Aayan-Mem/Memory-&-Context/ The Single Source of Truth Agents often hallucinate file paths or library versions because they lack a high-level map of the project. The Recall Engine solves this by maintaining five core truth-files: File Ground Truth Role App-Goal.md Defines exactly what the product is and its core value proposition. Stack.md Maps every framework, language, and directory to its specific role. Context-index.md A directory of preferences, API locations, and documentation pointers. Design-Guidelines.md Stores visual constraints (colors, animations, radii) to ensure UI consistency. rules.md Measurable code quality thresholds and architectural "Do/Don't" rules. Contradiction Scanner When the agent updates a memory file, it must run the validate tool. This scanner detects conflicting facts across the Recall Engine—for example, if Stack.md references a library that rules.md explicitly forbids. node a-mem validate Anti-Drift Headers To prevent information rot, every Recall Engine file must include standardized metadata headers. The agent tracks the last modified session to ensure the context remains fresh. **Last-Modified-By:** Antigravity **Last-Modified-Session:** 12 Previous Accountability Engine Next Stopping Hallucinations

title: Operational Rules
content: Operational Rules — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K The 8 Operational Rules To ensure a zero-hallucination environment and maintain architectural integrity, all AI agents must strictly adhere to the following 8 operational rules at all times. Enforcement: Failure to follow these rules will result in an immediate "Logic Drift" warning and potentially invalid architectural decisions. # Rule Name Operational Purpose 1 Cold Start Run node a-mem coldstart at the beginning of every session to sync with project context. 2 Contradiction Check Run node a-mem validate after any update to Memory files to detect conflicting facts. 3 Complexity Tiers Classify tasks into Tier 1 (trivial), Tier 2 (logged), or Tier 3 (matrix required). 4 Session Management Use session-counter.json and the canonical template for consistent decision logging. 5 Hallucination Guard Run node a-mem verify to prove referenced file paths exist before committing to a change. 6 Proof-of-Read Echo 3 specific facts from the coldstart briefing to prove context absorption. 7 Pre-Commit Checklist Perform a post-implementation verification to ensure the code matches the decision log. 8 Zero Token Leaks Never read full files. Use the find tool for surgical snippet extraction only. Enforcement & Accountability These rules are not mere suggestions; they are the protocol that keeps the agent tethered to the reality of the codebase. By following this protocol, the agent transforms from a "code generator" into a "verifiable reasoning engine." Previous Command List Start Over Introduction

title: Security & Privacy
content: Security & Privacy — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Security & Privacy Data sovereignty is at the heart of .Aayan-Mem . Unlike cloud-based memory solutions, your data never leaves your environment. Local-First Architecture .Aayan-Mem operates entirely on your local machine. It does not require: Cloud vector databases (Pinecone, Chroma, etc.) Third-party embedding APIs Network access of any kind Zero-Telemetry Guarantee The framework contains no tracking scripts, no telemetry, and no hidden pings. The CLI is pure Node.js that interacts only with your .Aayan-Mem/ directory. Enterprise Integration For enterprise teams, this means .Aayan-Mem is safe for use with: Proprietary IP: Your architectural decisions and business logic remain in your Git repo. Air-gapped Environments: Since it has zero dependencies, it can be deployed in secure, offline environments. Compliance: Easily meets GDPR, SOC2, and HIPAA requirements as no data is processed by third-party servers. Note: We recommend adding .Aayan-Mem/Agent/session-counter.json to your .gitignore if you are working in a team to prevent merge conflicts on session IDs. Previous Token Optimization Next Agent Integration

title: Token Optimization
content: Token Optimization — .Aayan-Mem Docs Search... Ctrl K Token Optimization Context window management is the most expensive part of AI coding. .Aayan-Mem is engineered to reduce token consumption by up to 90% compared to traditional methods. The Problem: Token Bloat Most agents read entire files to find a single function. In a 500-line file, that's roughly 2,000-4,000 tokens wasted every time the file is accessed. Over a long project, this leads to context exhaustion and massive API bills. The Surgical Approach The node a-mem find "query" command uses a Context Buffer Strategy : Surgical Hits: It only returns lines that match your query. Fixed Window: It adds exactly 2 lines above and 2 lines below the hit (4 lines total buffer). Deduplication: If multiple hits occur in the same area, it merges the buffers to prevent redundant text. Comparison Table Method Avg. Tokens (500 Line File) Efficiency Standard "Read File" 4,000 0% Basic RAG Chunking 800 - 1,200 75% .Aayan-Mem Find 50 - 150 97% Note: By reducing noise, you also increase the AI's "Reasoning Accuracy" because it isn't distracted by irrelevant code. Previous Best Practices Next Security & Privacy
